commonMistakes: Expecting the same effect from topical HA as from oral HA — topical HA primarily sits on the skin surface and provides surface hydration; oral HA reaches deeper dermal layers and synovial fluid.
Using high-molecular-weight HA products for oral use — large HA molecules are poorly absorbed; low-MW or enzymatically degraded forms are needed for effective oral bioavailability.

iron.tagline: Essential mineral for oxygen transport, energy, and immune defence
iron.primaryUse: Core component of haemoglobin and myoglobin; required for oxygen transport and mitochondrial energy production
iron.overview: Iron deficiency is the single most common nutritional deficiency worldwide, affecting roughly 25% of the global population. Iron is the functional centre of haemoglobin — without adequate iron, red blood cells cannot carry sufficient oxygen, producing the fatigue, shortness of breath, and cognitive impairment characteristic of anaemia. Iron bisglycinate (also called iron glycinate or Ferrochel) is the best-tolerated and most bioavailable supplemental form, causing far less constipation and GI irritation than cheaper ferrous sulfate. Women of reproductive age, athletes, and plant-based eaters are at highest risk.
iron.typicalDose: 18–45mg elemental iron daily (therapeutic: 100–200mg under medical supervision)
bestFor: Endurance athletes (foot-strike haemolysis and sweat losses)
Vegans and vegetarians (non-haem iron is less bioavailable)
keyFacts: Iron bisglycinate has 2–4× greater absorption than ferrous sulfate and causes significantly fewer GI side effects — worth the higher cost
Vitamin C dramatically enhances non-haem iron absorption — always take with citrus juice or a Vitamin C supplement
Never supplement iron without confirming deficiency via blood test — iron overload (haemochromatosis) is dangerous and symptoms can resemble deficiency
commonMistakes: Self-supplementing without a blood test — iron overload is as dangerous as deficiency. Always confirm serum ferritin and transferrin saturation before starting iron supplementation.
Taking iron with calcium, coffee, or tea — calcium competes with iron for absorption; tannins in tea and coffee inhibit non-haem iron uptake by up to 60%. Take iron away from these.

folate.tagline: Essential B vitamin for DNA synthesis, cell division, and neural development
folate.primaryUse: Acts as a methyl group carrier in one-carbon metabolism, essential for DNA synthesis, red blood cell formation, and homocysteine regulation
folate.overview: Folate (Vitamin B9) is one of the most critical nutrients in human biology — it is essential for DNA synthesis, cell division, and the methylation reactions that regulate gene expression. Its importance during early pregnancy for preventing neural tube defects (spina bifida) is so well established that fortification of flour is mandated in over 80 countries. As a supplement, the active form L-methylfolate (5-MTHF) bypasses the MTHFR enzyme step that up to 40% of people have reduced activity in — making it far superior to folic acid for those with this common genetic variant.
folate.typicalDose: 400–800mcg daily (800mcg–1mg for pregnancy or MTHFR variants)
bestFor: Pregnancy and pre-conception (neural tube defect prevention)
keyFacts: Up to 40% of people have MTHFR variants that reduce conversion of folic acid to the active form — L-methylfolate (5-MTHF) bypasses this step entirely
The neural tube closes within 28 days of conception — supplementation must begin before pregnancy is confirmed, not after
Works synergistically with B12 — both are required for the homocysteine-to-methionine conversion; deficiency in either raises cardiovascular risk
commonMistakes: Using folic acid instead of methylfolate if you have an MTHFR variant — folic acid requires enzymatic conversion that MTHFR-variant individuals do poorly; unmetabolised folic acid can accumulate and may have adverse effects.

Starting folate only after a positive pregnancy test — the neural tube closes in the first 28 days, often before pregnancy is known; women planning pregnancy should begin supplementing at least 1–3 months before conception.

biotin.tagline: The B vitamin behind healthy hair, skin, nails, and metabolism
biotin.primaryUse: Essential cofactor for five carboxylase enzymes involved in fatty acid synthesis, amino acid catabolism, and gluconeogenesis
biotin.overview: Biotin (Vitamin B7) is a water-soluble B vitamin that serves as the essential cofactor for five carboxylase enzymes critical to macronutrient metabolism. Clinical biotin deficiency is rare but produces unmistakable symptoms: hair thinning, brittle nails, and scaly skin rash. The supplement market around biotin — often at megadoses of 1000–10,000mcg — was built on its established role in hair and nail keratin synthesis. While megadose biotin is unlikely to benefit people without deficiency, those with subtly low biotin (frequent raw egg consumption, alcoholism, certain medications) show real improvements in hair and nail quality.
bestFor: Hair thinning associated with biotin deficiency
Individuals consuming raw eggs regularly (avidin blocks biotin absorption)
keyFacts: Raw egg whites contain avidin — a protein that binds biotin and prevents its absorption; regular raw egg consumption is a genuine cause of biotin deficiency
High-dose biotin (≥ 5mg) interferes with thyroid and troponin lab tests — stop supplementation 3–7 days before blood tests to avoid false readings
True biotin deficiency is rare in healthy adults eating cooked food; megadose supplementation likely only benefits those with genuine deficiency
commonMistakes: Not disclosing biotin supplementation before blood tests — high-dose biotin causes false thyroid (TSH, T4) and cardiac (troponin) results, which can lead to serious diagnostic errors. Stop 3–7 days before testing.

Expecting megadose biotin to grow hair significantly beyond baseline in non-deficient individuals — the popular belief that 5000–10,000mcg promotes hair growth in everyone is not supported by evidence; the benefit is specific to deficiency correction.

milk-thistle.tagline: The world's most studied herb for liver protection and detoxification
milk-thistle.primaryUse: Silymarin flavonoids stabilise hepatocyte membranes and act as powerful antioxidants, protecting liver cells from toxin-induced damage
milk-thistle.overview: Milk thistle (Silybum marianum) contains a group of flavonoid compounds collectively called silymarin, with silybin being the most biologically active. Silymarin exerts hepatoprotective effects through multiple mechanisms: stabilising liver cell membranes against toxin permeation, acting as a potent antioxidant that scavenges free radicals in hepatocytes, stimulating RNA polymerase I to accelerate liver cell regeneration, and reducing fibrotic activity in chronic liver disease. It has the longest evidence base of any hepatoprotective herb and is recommended by liver specialists in several European countries for alcoholic liver disease and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease.
milk-thistle.typicalDose: 140mg silymarin (standardised to 70–80%) two to three times daily
bestFor: Liver support during alcohol consumption or medication use
Post-cycle therapy stacks for performance-drug users
keyFacts: Standardisation is critical — products should state silymarin content (70–80% standardised extract), not just milk thistle seed powder which has variable and low silymarin content
Phytosome forms (Siliphos®) have 4–10× greater bioavailability than standard silymarin due to enhanced absorption
Protective effect has been shown against Amanita phalloides (death cap mushroom) toxicity — the pharmacological basis is well established, not anecdotal
commonMistakes: Buying non-standardised milk thistle seed powder — silymarin content in raw powder varies enormously and is typically very low; always choose products standardised to 70–80% silymarin.
Expecting it to undo liver damage from alcohol consumed immediately before — milk thistle works prophylactically and through chronic supplementation, not as a same-day recovery agent.

elderberry.tagline: Clinically validated berry extract for faster cold and flu recovery
elderberry.primaryUse: Flavonoid compounds in elderberry inhibit viral entry and replication while modulating immune cytokine production
elderberry.overview: Black elderberry (Sambucus nigra) is one of the few herbal remedies with genuine randomised controlled trial data for its primary indication — reducing the severity and duration of upper respiratory infections. Elderberry's anthocyanins have been shown in vitro and in clinical trials to inhibit viral haemagglutinin proteins, blocking viral attachment to host cells, and to stimulate cytokine production that coordinates immune response. Meta-analyses consistently show reductions in cold duration of 1–2 days when supplementation begins at symptom onset.
elderberry.typicalDose: 600–900mg elderberry extract daily at symptom onset; 150–300mg for prevention
bestFor: Reducing cold and flu duration when taken at first symptoms
Frequent travellers and those in high-exposure environments
keyFacts: Most effective when started at the first sign of symptoms — prophylactic use shows smaller benefits than early therapeutic use
Raw elderberries are toxic (contain cyanogenic glycosides) — only use processed extracts, syrups, or supplements, never raw berries
Quality standardisation matters — look for products standardised to anthocyanin content; many elderberry gummies contain negligible active compounds
commonMistakes: Consuming raw elderberries or unprocessed elderberry products — raw elderberries and leaves contain sambunigrin (a cyanogenic glycoside) that causes nausea and vomiting; only use heat-processed extracts.

Taking elderberry syrup products with high sugar content — many commercial syrups contain more sugar than elderberry; this may blunt immune benefit and is counterproductive for those managing blood sugar.

tongkat-ali.tagline: Southeast Asian adaptogen for testosterone, energy, and stress resilience
tongkat-ali.primaryUse: Reduces cortisol and supports the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis, leading to improved testosterone levels in stressed or sub-optimal conditions
tongkat-ali.overview: Tongkat Ali (Eurycoma longifolia) has been used for centuries in Malaysian and Indonesian traditional medicine as a tonic for male vitality and energy. Modern research has focused on its ability to reduce sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) and lower cortisol — both of which increase free testosterone. The standardised extract LJ100® (200:1 root extract) has the strongest clinical backing, with multiple trials showing significant testosterone increases in men with late-onset hypogonadism, chronic stress, and physically demanding training. Its effects are most pronounced in those with below-optimal testosterone, rather than those already at the high end of normal.
bestFor: Male athletes in heavy training blocks (overtraining)
keyFacts: LJ100® is the most clinically validated extract — it is a 200:1 water extract standardised to glycosaponins, eurypeptides, and polysaccharides; generic tongkat ali root powder is not equivalent
Effects are cortisol-dependent — it works best in stressed individuals; already low-cortisol individuals see smaller testosterone shifts
Also used in female protocols for libido and energy — not exclusive to men, though most data is in male populations
commonMistakes: Using unverified bulk powder instead of standardised extract — the ratio of tongkat ali root extract (100:1, 200:1) dramatically affects active compound concentration; cheap powders may contain negligible eurypeptides.
Expecting rapid testosterone shifts within days — meaningful hormonal changes from tongkat ali require 4–8 weeks of consistent supplementation to become statistically and physiologically significant.

maca-root.tagline: Andean adaptogen for energy, libido, and hormonal balance
maca-root.primaryUse: Non-hormonal adaptogen that modulates hypothalamic function to support libido, fertility, energy, and endocrine balance
maca-root.overview: Maca (Lepidium meyenii) is a cruciferous root vegetable grown at high altitudes in the Peruvian Andes that has been used as food and medicine for over 2000 years. Unlike many libido supplements, maca does not directly alter sex hormone levels — it appears to work through the hypothalamus, modulating the endocrine system upstream. Clinical trials show consistent improvements in sexual desire in men and women, improved sperm quality in men, and reductions in menopausal symptoms (hot flushes, mood changes) in women. Black maca has the strongest evidence for fertility and cognition, while red maca is better studied for prostate health.
bestFor: Libido and sexual function (men and women)
keyFacts: Gelatinised (cooked/pre-gelatinised) maca has improved digestibility and starch removal — raw maca can cause GI discomfort and has less bioavailable macamides
Colour matters clinically — black maca: best for cognition and male fertility; red maca: best for prostate health and female hormonal balance; yellow: general purpose
Does not raise oestrogen or testosterone directly — safe for oestrogen-sensitive conditions unlike many libido supplements
commonMistakes: Using raw maca powder instead of gelatinised — raw maca contains high starch content that can cause GI distress, and its macamide content is less bioavailable; gelatinised (heat-processed) maca is the preferred supplemental form.
Expecting immediate libido effects — maca's adaptogenic action builds over weeks; studies showing libido improvements typically run for 6–12 weeks before meaningful effects emerge.

vitamin-a.tagline: Essential fat-soluble vitamin for vision, immunity, and cellular growth
vitamin-a.primaryUse: Required for rhodopsin synthesis in photoreceptors, immune cell differentiation, and regulation of gene expression via retinoic acid receptors
vitamin-a.overview: Vitamin A encompasses two distinct forms: preformed retinol (from animal foods) and provitamin A carotenoids like beta-carotene (from plant foods). Retinol is biologically active immediately; beta-carotene must be converted to retinol in the intestinal wall — a process that is genetically variable and inefficient in some individuals. Vitamin A is essential for low-light vision (rhodopsin), immune T-cell development, skin and mucosal barrier integrity, and reproductive function. Deficiency causes night blindness and severe immune impairment. Supplemental vitamin A requires care as it is one of the fat-soluble vitamins that accumulates and can reach toxic levels.
vitamin-a.typicalDose: 700–900mcg RAE (2300–3000 IU) daily; do not exceed 3000mcg RAE (10,000 IU) from retinol
bestFor: Night vision and eye health maintenance
keyFacts: Beta-carotene (plant-sourced) is not toxic at high doses — only preformed retinol (animal-sourced/supplements) accumulates and can cause toxicity
Up to 45% of people are poor converters of beta-carotene to retinol due to BCMO1 gene variants — these individuals must get preformed retinol from diet or supplements
Vitamin A and Vitamin D work synergistically — Vitamin D receptors require retinoic acid for optimal expression; they compete at high doses and should be balanced
commonMistakes: Taking high-dose preformed retinol supplements when dietary intake is already adequate — preformed vitamin A accumulates in the liver and is the most common cause of fat-soluble vitamin toxicity; beta-carotene is far safer for supplementation.

Assuming plant-based diets provide adequate vitamin A via beta-carotene — poor converters (a common genetic variant) can have very low retinol status despite high beta-carotene intake; checking retinol blood levels is advisable for vegans.

vitamin-e.tagline: Fat-soluble antioxidant that protects cell membranes and supports immune function
vitamin-e.primaryUse: Acts as a chain-breaking antioxidant in lipid membranes, preventing oxidative cascade damage to polyunsaturated fatty acids
vitamin-e.overview: Vitamin E is a family of eight related compounds — four tocopherols and four tocotrienols — with alpha-tocopherol being the most biologically active and the form that the human body preferentially maintains. Its primary function is to protect cell membranes and circulating lipoproteins from oxidative damage by intercepting free radical chain reactions in lipid bilayers. It also plays roles in immune signalling and platelet aggregation inhibition. The caveat: high-dose supplemental vitamin E (above 400 IU/day) has shown adverse effects in some large trials, including increased mortality risk — reinforcing that whole-food tocopherols and modest supplementation are preferable to megadoses.
vitamin-e.typicalDose: 15mg (22 IU natural, 33 IU synthetic) daily; do not exceed 1000mg (1500 IU)
bestFor: Cardiovascular protection in combination with other antioxidants
keyFacts: Natural d-alpha-tocopherol is significantly more bioavailable than synthetic dl-alpha-tocopherol — look for the 'd-' prefix, not 'dl-'
Full-spectrum tocopherol (mixed tocopherols including gamma-tocopherol) is more physiologically relevant than isolated alpha-tocopherol alone; high-dose alpha-T displaces gamma-T
Vitamin E and K2 interact — high vitamin E can antagonise vitamin K-dependent clotting factors; those on anticoagulants should seek medical guidance
commonMistakes: Using high-dose isolated alpha-tocopherol (400+ IU) — large RCTs including HOPE-TOO show increased adverse outcomes at high doses. Whole-food or mixed tocopherol sources at physiological doses are safer.

Buying synthetic dl-alpha-tocopherol products — synthetic vitamin E has roughly 50% the biological activity of natural d-alpha-tocopherol for the same stated IU dose; natural forms are materially superior.

astaxanthin.tagline: The most potent antioxidant carotenoid in nature
astaxanthin.primaryUse: Crosses cell membranes to quench singlet oxygen and neutralise free radicals throughout the entire lipid bilayer — inside and outside simultaneously
astaxanthin.overview: Astaxanthin is a xanthophyll carotenoid that gives salmon, flamingos, and shrimp their pink-red colour. What makes it remarkable is its molecular structure: it spans the entire width of cell membranes and can quench free radicals on both the interior and exterior simultaneously — a property no other antioxidant carotenoid possesses. Animal studies consistently demonstrate extraordinary antioxidant potency. Human trials show benefits for exercise recovery (reduced oxidative stress and inflammation), skin photoprotection and elasticity, eye health, and cardiovascular markers. Bioavailability is poor without fat — always take with a meal containing fat.
bestFor: Exercise recovery and reduced muscle damage from oxidative stress
Skin health and UV protection from within
keyFacts: Natural astaxanthin from Haematococcus pluvialis algae is considered superior to synthetic astaxanthin, which consists of different isomers with lower biological activity
Fat-soluble and requires dietary fat for absorption — take with a meal; taking it fasted dramatically reduces blood levels
Among carotenoids, it cannot be converted to vitamin A — this means no risk of vitamin A toxicity, even at higher doses
commonMistakes: Taking it on an empty stomach — astaxanthin is highly fat-soluble and poorly absorbed without dietary fat; always take it with a meal containing fat to achieve meaningful plasma levels.

Buying synthetic astaxanthin to save cost — synthetic astaxanthin contains racemic isomers not found in natural sources, which have lower antioxidant activity; natural algae-derived astaxanthin is the research-validated form.

iodine.tagline: Essential trace mineral that is the backbone of thyroid hormone production
iodine.primaryUse: Required for biosynthesis of the thyroid hormones T3 and T4, which regulate metabolism, energy production, and development
iodine.overview: Iodine is incorporated directly into thyroid hormones — thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3) — making it the only mineral with a structural rather than cofactor role in hormone synthesis. Iodine deficiency is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disability globally and produces goitre, hypothyroidism, and severe developmental impairment in foetuses when deficient in pregnant women. Despite iodised salt programmes, deficiency has re-emerged in many populations following dietary shifts away from iodised salt and dairy, increased use of non-iodised sea salt, and vegan diets (limited seafood and dairy). Supplemental iodine must be dosed carefully — excess can also disrupt thyroid function.
iodine.typicalDose: 150mcg daily (RDA); 220mcg during pregnancy; do not routinely exceed 1100mcg
bestFor: Vegan and plant-based eaters avoiding seaweed and dairy
keyFacts: Seaweed is rich in iodine but concentration varies dramatically by species and harvest region — kelp supplements can deliver unpredictably high doses and are not reliable for precise dosing
Selenium and iodine work together — selenium deficiency impairs iodine utilisation; ensure selenium is adequate alongside iodine supplementation
Excess iodine (Wolff-Chaikoff effect) can paradoxically suppress thyroid function — stay within established safe upper limits
commonMistakes: Using kelp as an iodine supplement — kelp iodine content varies from trace amounts to dangerously high concentrations between batches; potassium iodide tablets provide precise and predictable dosing.
Supplementing iodine without adequate selenium — selenium is required for iodine metabolism (as selenodeiodinase enzymes); iodine without selenium in deficiency states can worsen thyroid dysfunction.

chromium.tagline: Trace mineral that enhances insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism
chromium.primaryUse: Potentiates insulin receptor signalling, improving glucose uptake and reducing blood sugar fluctuations after meals
chromium.overview: Chromium is an essential trace mineral that enhances the activity of insulin — the key hormone that shuttles glucose into cells. Its proposed mechanism involves chromodulin, a chromium-binding oligopeptide that amplifies insulin receptor tyrosine kinase activity. Clinical evidence for chromium picolinate (the best-absorbed supplemental form) shows modest but real reductions in fasting blood glucose and HbA1c in people with type 2 diabetes and insulin resistance. Effects in healthy, insulin-sensitive individuals are much smaller and inconsistent. It is most relevant for those with blood sugar regulation challenges.
bestFor: Carbohydrate cravings and blood sugar control on hypocaloric diets
keyFacts: Chromium picolinate is substantially better absorbed than chromium chloride or chromium polynicotinate — always check the form
The evidence base is strongest for type 2 diabetics and those with impaired glucose tolerance; healthy individuals with normal insulin sensitivity see minimal benefit
Processed foods deplete dietary chromium — refined sugar consumption increases urinary chromium losses, creating a vicious cycle for blood sugar regulation
commonMistakes: Using chromium chloride instead of chromium picolinate — chloride is very poorly absorbed; picolinate is the form used in the vast majority of positive clinical trials.

Expecting chromium to substitute for dietary change — chromium improves insulin signal quality but cannot compensate for chronically high sugar or refined carbohydrate intake; it works best as an adjunct to a lower-glycaemic diet.

saw-palmetto.tagline: Plant extract for prostate health and DHT management
saw-palmetto.primaryUse: Inhibits 5-alpha reductase, reducing conversion of testosterone to DHT, to support prostate health and manage androgenic hair loss
saw-palmetto.overview: Saw palmetto (Serenoa repens) is derived from the berries of a native North American palm. Its primary biological mechanism is inhibition of 5-alpha reductase — the enzyme that converts testosterone to the more potent androgen dihydrotestosterone (DHT). DHT is the primary driver of both benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) and androgenic alopecia. Clinical trials for BPH symptoms show saw palmetto reduces urinary frequency and improves urinary flow comparably to tamsulosin (an alpha-blocker drug) in mild-to-moderate BPH. Evidence for hair loss is more modest but emerging.
bestFor: Prostate health maintenance in men over 45
keyFacts: The liposterolic extract (fat-soluble, typically hexane-extracted) is the form with clinical evidence; water extracts and powders lack the active fatty acid and phytosterol content
Takes 4–8 weeks minimum for BPH symptom improvements — do not assess effectiveness before 2–3 months of consistent use
Does not lower PSA (prostate-specific antigen) the way finasteride does — important for prostate cancer screening, as PSA levels remain informative under saw palmetto
commonMistakes: Using non-standardised saw palmetto berry powder — the whole berry powder lacks the concentrated fatty acid content of liposterolic extract; look specifically for products standardised to 85–95% fatty acids.

Expecting it to fully reverse established hair loss — saw palmetto may slow DHT-mediated hair loss and is most effective as a preventive measure; established alopecia requires more aggressive intervention alongside it.

lutein-zeaxanthin.tagline: Macular pigments that protect eyes from blue light and age-related degeneration
lutein-zeaxanthin.primaryUse: Concentrated in the macula and lens, these carotenoids filter high-energy blue light and quench singlet oxygen to protect photoreceptors
lutein-zeaxanthin.overview: Lutein and zeaxanthin are the only dietary carotenoids selectively concentrated in the macula — the central region of the retina responsible for high-acuity vision. They form the macular pigment, which functions as a natural blue-light filter and antioxidant shield for photoreceptors. The landmark AREDS2 trial established lutein + zeaxanthin supplementation as a standard of care for reducing progression of intermediate age-related macular degeneration (AMD) to advanced AMD by ~25%. Emerging evidence also supports benefits for visual performance in high screen-time environments, reducing glare sensitivity and improving contrast sensitivity.
bestFor: Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) prevention and progression reduction
High screen-time users and digital eye strain
Those with a family history of AMD or macular disease
keyFacts: The AREDS2 trial — the largest and most rigorous eye supplement trial ever conducted — replaced beta-carotene with lutein/zeaxanthin in the formula due to superior safety and efficacy
The 5:1 lutein-to-zeaxanthin ratio mirrors the natural macular pigment composition — products deviating significantly from this ratio are less physiologically optimised
Dark green vegetables (kale, spinach, collards) are the richest food sources, but dietary amounts rarely reach the 10mg lutein threshold used in the AREDS2 trial
commonMistakes: Taking beta-carotene supplements simultaneously — high-dose beta-carotene competes with lutein and zeaxanthin for intestinal absorption and was removed from the updated AREDS2 formula for this reason; avoid combining them.

Using products with very low zeaxanthin content — many cheap lutein supplements contain only trace zeaxanthin despite its distinct and important role in the central macula; look for at least 2mg zeaxanthin alongside 10mg lutein.
